my_sentence: I use it to make the air more fresher.
correction: I use it to make the air much fresher.
explanation: 'Fresher' is already a comparative, so you can't put 'more' before it. To intensify a comparative, use 'much' (much fresher), not 'more'.
correct_examples: This room feels much fresher with the window open.
The mountain air is much cleaner than the city air.

my_sentence: The man always gives you some second guesses when you made some mistakes.
correction: The man always second-guesses you whenever you make some mistakes.
explanation: 'Second-guess' is a transitive verb: you 'second-guess someone', not 'give someone second guesses'. Also, for a habitual truth use the present tense 'when you make', not 'when you made'.
correct_examples: Stop second-guessing yourself and trust your decision.
My boss second-guesses every choice I make.

my_sentence: The plane won't take off until the rain stopped.
correction: The plane won't take off until the rain stops.
explanation: In a time clause introduced by 'until', use the present simple to refer to the future, not the past: 'until the rain stops'.
correct_examples: We'll wait here until the bus comes.
Don't leave until I tell you.

my_sentence: I don't believe buying lottery tickets can make a lot of money.
correction: I don't believe buying lottery tickets can help me make a lot of money.
explanation: Lottery tickets don't 'make money' by themselves; the natural phrasing is that something 'helps you make money'. Add 'help me' so a person is the one making the money.
correct_examples: A side job can help you make extra money.
Good investments help people build wealth.

my_sentence: Even if you get a lot of money, you can't control yourself.
correction: Even if you get a lot of money, you will not be able to control yourself.
explanation: To talk about future ability, use 'will (not) be able to', not the present 'can't'. 'Can' has no future form, so 'will be able to' fills that role.
correct_examples: After the surgery, you won't be able to drive for a week.
If you save now, you'll be able to buy a house later.

my_sentence: Maybe half a year or one year later, you become poor.
correction: Maybe half a year or a year later, you will become poor.
explanation: A future consequence needs 'will': 'you will become poor', not the present 'you become poor'.
correct_examples: If you keep spending like this, you will go broke.
One day you will regret it.

my_sentence: When they become rich, it's from a step to a step.
correction: They become rich step by step.
explanation: The fixed adverbial phrase is 'step by step' (gradually). 'From a step to a step' is not idiomatic English.
correct_examples: She learned the language step by step.
We'll solve this step by step.

my_sentence: You earned the money from hard working.
correction: You earned the money from working hard.
explanation: Here you need the verb phrase 'working hard' (verb + adverb). 'Hard working' as two words isn't a noun; 'hardworking' (one word) is an adjective describing a person.
correct_examples: He earned everything by working hard.
She's a hardworking student.

my_sentence: We need to use unclear energy to stabilize them.
correction: We need to use nuclear energy to stabilize them.
explanation: You repeatedly said 'unclear' (= not clear) when you meant 'nuclear' (/ˈnjuːkliər/). Watch the syllable order: nu-cle-ar, not un-clear.
correct_examples: Nuclear energy provides a stable power supply.
The country is investing in nuclear power.

my_sentence: He invest all of his money in it and get nothing.
correction: He invested all his money in it and got nothing. / He invests all his money in it and gets nothing.
explanation: Keep the verb forms consistent and don't leave them bare: in the present add the third-person -s ('he invests / he gets'); for a finished story use the past ('he invested / he got').
correct_examples: He spends everything he earns and saves nothing.
She invested all her savings and lost it all.

my_sentence: The man who teaches the student in college looks so kind.
correction: The man who teaches students in college looks so kind.
explanation: When you mean students in general (more than one), use the bare plural 'students', not 'the student', which points to one specific person.
correct_examples: She teaches students from all over the world.
He helps students improve their writing.

my_sentence: He is very kind to his managers, but to his members he is very terrible.
correction: He is very kind to his managers, but he treats them terribly.
explanation: 'Be terrible to someone' is weak; the natural verb is 'treat someone terribly/badly'. Use 'treat + object + adverb'.
correct_examples: He treats his employees badly.
She treats everyone with respect.

my_sentence: He is very kind to his managers, and he treats his members terribly.
correction: He is very kind to his managers, but he treats his members terribly.
explanation: Two clauses that contrast should be joined with 'but', not 'and'. 'And' just adds information; 'but' signals the opposite.
correct_examples: The work is hard, but it pays well.
She studied a lot, but she still failed.

my_sentence: He spent a lot of money buying materials he needed in the work.
correction: He spent a lot of money buying materials he needed at work.
explanation: Use 'at work' to mean 'at one's job/workplace'. 'In the work' is not idiomatic for this meaning.
correct_examples: I use this software at work every day.
She got injured at work.

my_sentence: We want to provide enough jobs for unemployment people.
correction: We want to provide enough jobs for unemployed people.
explanation: 'Unemployment' is a noun (the state of having no job); to describe people you need the adjective 'unemployed'.
correct_examples: The program helps unemployed people find work.
Unemployment fell last year.

my_sentence: The boy was the most conceited in his class.
correction: The boy was the most conceited person in his class.
explanation: After a superlative adjective you usually need a noun: 'the most conceited person', not just 'the most conceited'. The noun says what kind of thing is the most X.
correct_examples: She is the smartest student in the room.
It was the most expensive meal I've ever had.

my_sentence: Reading more books can help us improve understanding through discourse.
correction: Reading more books can help us improve our understanding through discourse.
explanation: Use a possessive 'our' before 'understanding' to show whose understanding improves. English usually marks the possessor here rather than leaving the noun bare.
correct_examples: Travel broadens our understanding of the world.
This will improve your understanding of the topic.

my_sentence: Enough money means they can live comfortable.
correction: Enough money means they can live comfortably.
explanation: Use an adverb ('comfortably') to modify the verb 'live', not the adjective 'comfortable'. Adverbs describe how an action is done.
correct_examples: They live comfortably on one salary.
He spoke confidently in the meeting.

my_sentence: Everyone need to make a plan their future.
correction: Everyone needs to make a plan for their own future.
explanation: 'Everyone' takes a singular verb, so it's 'everyone needs'. Also 'make a plan' needs 'for' before what the plan is about: 'a plan for their future'.
correct_examples: Everyone needs a break sometimes.
She made a plan for the weekend.

my_sentence: There are too many pets, so your neighborhoods are noisy.
correction: There are too many pets, so your neighbors are noisy.
explanation: 'Neighborhood' is the area/place you live in; 'neighbors' are the people living near you. People (not places) are the ones who are noisy.
correct_examples: My neighbors are very friendly.
It's a quiet neighborhood.

my_sentence: You will be never satisfied.
correction: You will never be satisfied.
explanation: The adverb 'never' goes after the auxiliary 'will' and before the main verb 'be': 'will never be'. Don't place it after 'be'.
correct_examples: I will never forget this.
He will never be late again.

my_sentence: There are many parents who love their children as me.
correction: There are many parents who love their children as much as I do.
explanation: To compare degree, use the 'as ... as' structure with a clause: 'as much as I do', not just 'as me'.
correct_examples: She works as hard as he does.
Nobody loves this team as much as I do.

my_sentence: I never met an orphanage before.
correction: I have never been to an orphanage before.
explanation: For an experience up to now, use the present perfect: 'I have never been to ...'. Also, you 'go to / have been to' a place, you don't 'meet' a place.
correct_examples: I have never been to Japan.
Have you ever been to a concert?

my_sentence: If it's not affect me, I don't care about it.
correction: If it doesn't affect me, I don't care about it.
explanation: 'Affect' is a main verb, so negate it with 'does not' + base form: 'it doesn't affect me'. You can't combine 'is' with another main verb ('is not affect').
correct_examples: If it doesn't work, call me.
This decision doesn't affect you.

my_sentence: You will not have time to accompany with them.
correction: You won't have time to accompany them.
explanation: 'Accompany' is transitive and takes a direct object with no preposition: 'accompany them', not 'accompany with them'.
correct_examples: I'll accompany you to the airport.
A nurse accompanied the patient.

my_sentence: If you spend more time on your work, you will don't have enough time to do other things.
correction: If you spend more time on your work, you will not have enough time to do other things.
explanation: After the modal 'will', use the base verb directly: 'will not have'. Don't insert 'don't' ('will don't' is impossible).
correct_examples: I will not be there tomorrow.
She will not have time this week.

my_sentence: I spend most of my time to work.
correction: I spend most of my time at work.
explanation: To say where you spend time, use 'at work'. 'To work' would express purpose, which isn't your meaning here.
correct_examples: He spends most of his day at work.
I'm at work until six.

my_sentence: If I sleep for three hours, the next day I will feel terrible and no energy to do anything.
correction: If I sleep for three hours, the next day I will feel terrible and have no energy to do anything.
explanation: Each part joined by 'and' needs its own verb. 'No energy' has no verb, so add 'have': 'feel terrible and have no energy'.
correct_examples: I'm tired and have no time to cook.
She felt sick and had no appetite.

my_sentence: I need to eat a sleeping pill to help me sleep.
correction: I need to take a sleeping pill to help me sleep.
explanation: The verb that goes with medicine, pills and tablets is 'take', not 'eat': 'take a pill', 'take medicine'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: Take this medicine twice a day.
He takes a sleeping pill before bed.
